subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
- **1** chessboard (8×8 grid of alternating light and dark squares)
- **16** white pieces: 1 King, 1 Queen, 2 Rooks, 2 Bishops, 2 Knights, 8 Pawns
- **16** black pieces: 1 King, 1 Queen, 2 Rooks, 2 Bishops, 2 Knights, 8 Pawns
- **Optional** — Chess clock for timed games

subtopics.title: Board Setup
subtopics.content:
1. **Orient board** — Place board so each player has a light square in their right-hand corner.
2. **Back rank (row 1 for White, row 8 for Black)** — From left to right: Rook, Knight, Bishop, Queen, King, Bishop, Knight, Rook.
3. **Queen placement** — Queen goes on her own color (white queen on light square, black queen on dark square).
4. **Pawns** — Place all 8 pawns on the second rank (row 2 for White, row 7 for Black).

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
- **White moves first** — The player with the white pieces always takes the first turn.
- **Determine colors** — Typically decided by mutual agreement, coin flip, or tournament pairing.
- **Clock settings** (if timed) — Set equal time for both players.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content: Chess is a two-player abstract strategy game played on an 8×8 board. The objective is to checkmate the opponent's King — placing it under attack with no legal escape.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
- Players alternate turns, starting with White.
- On each turn, a player must make exactly one legal move.
- A move consists of moving one piece to a new square according to that piece's movement rules.
- Capturing an opponent's piece removes it from the board.

subtopics.title: Piece Movement
subtopics.content:
#### King
- Moves exactly 1 square in any direction (horizontal, vertical, diagonal).
- Cannot move into check (a square attacked by an opponent's piece).

#### Queen
- Moves any number of squares in any direction (horizontal, vertical, diagonal).
- Cannot jump over other pieces.

#### Rook
- Moves any number of squares horizontally or vertically.
- Cannot jump over other pieces.

#### Bishop
- Moves any number of squares diagonally.
- Cannot jump over other pieces. Each bishop stays on its starting color.

#### Knight
- Moves in an L-shape: 2 squares in one direction + 1 square perpendicular.
- **Only piece that can jump** over other pieces.

#### Pawn
- Moves 1 square forward (toward opponent's side). Cannot move backward.
- **First move** — May optionally move 2 squares forward.
- **Captures** — Diagonally forward 1 square only.
- **Promotion** — Upon reaching the 8th rank, must promote to Queen, Rook, Bishop, or Knight.
- **En passant** — If an opponent's pawn advances 2 squares and lands beside your pawn, you may capture it as if it moved only 1 square (must be done immediately).

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Check
- A King is in check when attacked by an opponent's piece.
- The player in check must escape: move the King, block the attack, or capture the attacker.

#### Castling
- King moves 2 squares toward a Rook; that Rook jumps to the other side of the King.
- **Conditions**: Neither piece has moved; no pieces between them; King not in check, doesn't pass through or land in check.
- **Kingside** (short castle) — King goes 2 right, Rook goes 2-3 left.
- **Queenside** (long castle) — King goes 2 left, Rook goes 2-3 right.

#### Draw Conditions
- **Stalemate** — Player to move has no legal moves but is not in check.
- **Threefold repetition** — Same position occurs 3 times with same player to move.
- **50-move rule** — 50 consecutive moves by each player with no capture or pawn move.
- **Insufficient material** — Neither side can possibly checkmate (e.g., King vs King).
- **Agreement** — Both players agree to a draw.

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
#### Checkmate
- The King is in check and has no legal move to escape. The checkmating player wins.

#### Resignation
- A player may resign at any time, conceding the game.

#### Tournament Scoring
- Win: 1 point, Draw: 0.5 points, Loss: 0 points.

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Control the center** — Place pawns and pieces to control e4, d4, e5, d5.
- **Develop pieces early** — Move knights and bishops out before making many pawn moves.
- **Castle early** — Get your King to safety and connect your Rooks.
- **Don't bring the Queen out too early** — She can be chased by developing enemy pieces.
- **Count attackers vs defenders** — Before capturing, ensure you won't lose material.

subtopics.title: Key Strategies
subtopics.content:
- **Piece value** — Queen ≈ 9, Rook ≈ 5, Bishop ≈ 3, Knight ≈ 3, Pawn ≈ 1.
- **Forks and pins** — Use tactics to attack two pieces at once or pin pieces to the King.
- **Open files for Rooks** — Rooks are strongest on files with no pawns.
- **Bishop pair advantage** — Two bishops together are powerful on an open board.
- **Pawn structure** — Avoid doubled, isolated, and backward pawns when possible.
- **Endgame knowledge** — Learn basic King + Rook vs King and King + Pawn endgames.
